entry.title: Re: GRIR Account sustitution User exit
entry.content:
To troubleshoot, you can initiate debugging to verify whether the function module (FM) MR_ACCOUNT_ASSIGNMENT ("Determining the Account and Posting Key") is executed and at what point it is triggered.

  • Note: This FM includes enhancement points and customer exits, though these are not permitted for all transaction keys.
  • Additionally, review the standard account determination process (in the FM logic)

entry.title: Re: Multiple Cash Clearing Accounts with Single Account S...
entry.content:
Hello!

 First question: 

Account symbols use a masking mechanism to derive the GL account. For example, if the house bank GL account is 1000000 and the clearing account is 1000001, the account symbol mask would be set up as +++++++++1 — meaning the system takes the house bank's GL account and replaces the last character with "1".

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/automatic-supplier-payment-clearing-in-s-4hana-public-cloud/ba-p/12829129

Applied to this scenario:

House bank JPDP1/1EG2 is assigned to GL account 11001011

Account symbol mask +++++++080 takes the base GL account 11001011 and replaces the last 3 characters with 080, deriving 11001080.

The root cause of the error is a GL account range mismatch: the clearing account 11002080 sits in a different number range (11002xxx) than the house bank main account (11001xxx). The masking logic cannot bridge different GL account ranges using a single symbol,  it can only modify the suffix of the same base number.

To resolve this, exist two options:

Option 1: Align the clearing account number to the same base range as the house bank GL. If the main account is 11001011, create the clearing account as 11001080 and assign it correctly in GL master data as a Bank Subaccount linked to the reconciliation account. The mask +++++++080 will then resolve correctly.

Option 2: Define a dedicated account symbol with a fixed GL account (11002080 hardcoded, no masking) and assign it explicitly to the posting rule for this house bank. This removes the dynamic derivation entirely for this account but avoids renumbering.

 

Second question:  

A single account symbol with a mask cannot map different house bank accounts to different clearing accounts if those clearing accounts are in different GL number ranges. The mask applies uniformly across all house banks using that symbol.

When the bank reconciliation account is activated for the house bank account, the bank subaccount is derived by the account symbol. You must ensure the bank subaccount derived by the account symbol is properly created in GL account master data and assigned to the correct bank reconciliation account. Details here:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3376166

 For multiple house banks each requiring a different clearing account, the correct approach is:

Create separate account symbols per house bank clearing account (e.g. JPCLEAR1, JPCLEAR2), each with a fixed or appropriately masked GL account.Assign each account symbol to the relevant posting rule and external transaction type combination for the respective house bank. This avoids relying on a single mask to serve multiple incompatible GL ranges.

 

Third question:

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, posting rules are predefined and preconfigured and cannot be altered by customers. These rules are standardized to ensure consistency and compliance across the platform.  Creating a new posting rule is considered non-standard expert configuration. Details here: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3357476

The posting rule requires two posting areas to achieve both the GL posting and the AR subledger clearing simultaneously:

Posting Area 1 - posts to the bank GL account (debit bank, credit clearing account)

Posting Area 2 - clears the AR open item in the subledger

A second line must be added to the posting rule with Posting Area 2 (Subledger Accounting).  If Posting Area 2 is missing from the posting rule, the system posts to GL but does not trigger AR clearing.

The option for bank statement automation in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is the use of Processing Rules. Details here: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3374309

Processing Rules allow you to define matching logic (e.g. match bank statement line to open AR item by reference number, payment order number, or amount) without modifying locked posting rules.

 

References:

 SAP KBA 3376166 — House bank not assigned to GL account (FINS_ACDOC_POST133)

SAP KBA 3374309 — Posting and processing rules in bank statement posting — S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP KBA 3357476 — Creation of posting rules in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP KBA 2854415 — Information on bank statement posting rules in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

entry.title: Re: Credit Management for Professional Services Project B...
entry.content:
Hello!

There is no standard, direct integration between SAP Credit Management (FSCM) and the Project Billing process in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition at the Project Billing Request or Billing Document Request (BDR) level. The configuration activity "Activate Credit Management in Service Transactions" founded is specific to Service Management objects (service orders, service contracts) and does not apply to Professional services customer projects (scope items J11 / 4E9).

In the Professional Services scenario, there is always a 1:1 relationship between the Sales Order and the Project. The Sales Order is automatically created when the project stage is set to "contract preparation". The WBS billing element ID is stored in the sales order item. SAP Learning: https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-sales-intercompany-processes-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-professional-services/introducing-project-billing

Closest approach using standard:

Step 1. Activate FSCM credit management with sales order integration. 

Configure Credit Check rules: static check (open receivables + open orders) and/or overdue items check.

Assign the credit check to the relevant sales document types used by your customer projects.

Set the reaction to block the sales order (credit status set to "blocked") when the customer exceeds the limit or has overdue items above threshold.

Step 2: When a credit block is set on the sales order, the Billing Specialist working in "Manage Project Billing" will still be able to see the billing elements, but when attempting to release the BDR and convert it to a final invoice, the system will block the billing because the sales order item is credit-blocked. The credit block on the sales order effectively prevents the final invoice from being posted.

Step 3: Credit Release process. From a master data point of view, the Business Partner role UKM000 must be created for each relevant customer. Credit limit and credit segment data are maintained in the Business Partner master under the SAP Credit Management role. Credit managers can review and release blocked documents via the "My Documented Credit Decisions" Fiori app (equivalent of UKM_MY_DCDS). SAP Learning: https://learning.sap.com/courses/functions-innovations-in-sap-s-4hana-sales/working-with-sap-credit-management_d6b177ee-a8bb-4b77-80dc-87c04a9dd3f1

There are two important limitations compared to your original requirement:

  • The credit block is placed when the sales order is created or when a credit re-evaluation runs, not when the billing specialist processes the project billing request. The billing app itself does not trigger a real-time credit check. This means a customer could become overdue after the sales order is created, and the billing would not be automatically re-blocked.
  • The "Manage Project Billing" app does not provide the credit block. The billing specialist may not receive a meaningful error at the billing request step. The block becomes visible when attempting to create the final invoice/billing document, not upstream in the project billing workflow.

Overall configuration proposal:

Activate FSCM credit management and configure overdue item check and credit limit check for the sales document type used in your customer projects.

Schedule the credit re-evaluation job (available in "Schedule Credit Management Jobs" app) to run nightly or on a defined frequency, so that customers who become overdue after Sales Order creation are re-evaluated and blocked.

Define a credit release workflow so that the credit/finance team is notified and can approve or release blocked billing.

As a process control, add a credit status check to the period-end billing checklist so the billing specialist verifies there are no credit-blocked sales orders before releasing BDRs.

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI
entry.content:
"The iflow kafka topic routing  per domain is immutable. The metadata is the architecture."

Introduction

If you have worked with SAP PI/PO or even with SAP CPI IDoc routing, you already know the pattern: one receiver, one router, metadata drives the decision. The artifact never changes — the configuration does.

This blog will support you about IDOC:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice

The same principle applies to Kafka but when teams start integrating Kafka topics into SAP CPI, the most common approach creates one iFlow per topic, one deployment per event type, one change request per new business event. A Sales O2C domain with 5 event types becomes 5 iFlows, 5 deployments, and 5 maintenance points. When the topic naming changes or a new event is added — a developer is required.

This post presents a pattern that eliminates that overhead. One Kafka Sender Adapter. One router iFlow. One Value Mapping that grows. All iFlows inside the domain package are immutable.

This is not a workaround. This is ODCP applied to event-driven architecture in SAP CPI over the Domain Layer that lives above the tecnology.

The pattern was validated end-to-end with:

  • Confluent Cloud free tier as the Kafka broker
  • SAP BTP Integration Suite (CPI) standard Kafka Sender Adapter — no custom adapter, no additional cost
  • AVRO binary deserialization via Groovy Script using Apache Avro 1.12.1
  • Three input scenarios validated: raw bytes (production), Base64, and Hex (simulation/testing)
  • Confluent Wire Format detection (magic byte 0x00 + schema ID) handled automatically

⚠ This pattern is designed within the SDIA domain-centric governance framework using ODCP semantic name convention contract. If your organisation does not follow SDIA/ODCP, adapt the package names, iFlow names, and VM artifact names to your convention. The routing logic and Groovy script work independently of naming.

⚠ Scope Notice

The implementation demonstrated in this blog represents a foundational template — a minimal, working proof of concept designed to illustrate the immutability principle applied to Kafka topic routing in SAP CPI.

Schema design, field mapping, error handling strategy, retry policy, DLQ configuration, security hardening, and output format are intentionally kept simple. Every adaptation required for your specific landscape — domain model, event contract, operational SLA — is left to the implementing team.

The intent of this blog is not to deliver a production-ready solution. The intent is to demonstrate that one Kafka Sender Adapter, one router iFlow, and one Value Mapping are sufficient to implement a fully governed, domain-scoped, immutable routing mechanism in SAP CPI — without a single line of hardcode and without a custom adapter.

The principle is the deliverable. The implementation is your decision.

What is SDIA and why does it matter here ?

SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture) is a vendor-agnostic governance model built on one invariant: the business domain is the primary key of every artifact, from the API gateway to the data layer.

ODCP (Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern) is Layer 3 of SDIA and governs SAP BTP Integration Suite directly for SAP CI - Cloud Integration — one package per domain and subdomain ( other possible levels of granulality ), every artifact carries the domain token in a deterministic name, credentials segmented and etc..

In this pattern, the Kafka router iFlow is the domain boundary entry point — immutable, owned by the domain team, zero shared dependencies. The Value Mapping is the metadata contract. The processing iFlows are independently owned and deployed.

The same invariant applies at every layer of SDIA:

API Management  → GDCR/DDCR → KVM grows.     Proxy never changes.
SAP CPI IDoc    → ODCP      → VM grows.      iFlow never changes.
SAP CPI Kafka   → ODCP      → VM grows.      iFlow never changes.

The Architecture

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║             SDIA — Kafka Topic Domain Routing Architecture                       ║
║             Pattern: ODCP · Layer 3 · Sales O2C Domain                           ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
 LAYER 1 — KAFKA RECEIVER LAYER (Immutable · Zero Hardcode · Static iFlow)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

 Package : acme.sales.o2c.integrations
 iFlow   : id00.o2c.kafka.topic.routing.f.in.async
 VM      : id00.o2c.vm.metadata

 ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
 │         Confluent Cloud          │
 │                                  │
 │  acme.sales.o2c.order.created    │
 │  acme.sales.o2c.order.confirmed  │
 │  acme.sales.o2c.order.cancelled  │──────▶  Kafka Sender Adapter
 │  acme.sales.o2c.invoice.created  │         (all topics, one adapter)
 │  acme.sales.o2c.*                │
 └──────────────────────────────────┘
                                               │
                                               ▼
                               ┌───────────────────────────────┐
                               │         processData()         │
                               │                               │
                               │  reads kafka_receivedTopic    │
                               │  from message header          │
                               │                               │
                               │  key: acme.sales.o2c          │
                               │       .order.created          │
                               │                               │
                               │  VM lookup:                   │
                               │  id00.o2c.vm.metadata         │
                               │                               │
                               │  result: target_channel       │
                               └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                     ◇─────────────────◇
                                     │     Router      │
                                     ◇─────────────────◇
                                      │               │
                       is_mapped=true─┘               └─is_mapped=false
                      │                                      │
                      ▼                                      ▼
              ┌──────────────┐                     ┌─────────────────────────┐
              │     End      │                     │   buildErrorPayload()   │
              └──────┬───────┘                     │                         │
                     │                             │   kafka_topic           │
                     │ ProcessDirect               │   vm_artifact           │
                     ▼                             │   action_required       │
        ┌────────────────────────┐                 └────────────┬────────────┘
        │   ${target_channel}    │                              ▼
        └────────────┬───────────┘                 ┌────────────────────────┐
                     │                             │      ✗ Error End       │
                     ▼                             │        HTTP 500        │
 ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐└────────────────────────┘
 │                 acme.sales.o2c                 │
 │ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
 │ │ order.created   → /id01/.../created        │ │
 │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │
 │ │ order.confirmed → /id02/.../confirmed      │ │
 │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │
 │ │ order.cancelled → /id03/.../cancelled      │ │◀─── acme.sales.o2c.order.created
 │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │
 │ │ invoice.created → /id04/.../invoice-created│ │
 │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
 └───────────────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                             │
                             │ ProcessDirect internal
                             ▼

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
 LAYER 2 — DOMAIN PROCESSING LAYER (SDIA Governed · Independent Ownership)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

 Package : acme.sales.o2c.integrations
 iFlow   : id01.o2c.kafka.order.created  ← template · same pattern for all events
 
 ⚠ This iFlow is a template. The same structure applies to every subsequent
   processing iFlow in the domain — id01.o2c.kafka.order.confirmed,
   id01.o2c.kafka.invoice.created, and so on. Only the business logic inside
   changes. The routing mechanism, the adapter, and the router iFlow
   remain identical and untouched.

 ┌──────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │  ProcessDirect   │     │                Domain Processing                 │
 │  local listener  │────▶│                                                  │
 │                  │     │  · AVRO deserialization                          │
 └──────────────────┘     │  · message mapping                               │
                          │  · content enrichment                            │
                          │  · schema validation                             │
                          │  · retry policy    ← domain team defines SLA     │
                          │  · JMS queue       ← domain team defines         │
                          │  · DLQ             ← domain team defines         │
                          │  · alerting        ← domain team defines         │
                          │  · target adapter  (HTTP · SOAP · RFC · OData)   │
                          └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 The Receiver Layer is frozen. The Domain Layer evolves.
 A new event type never touches the router iFlow, 
only the external parameter regards the Domain and Subdomain a new possible topic.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Why This Is Not Overengineering

This question deserves an honest answer — because in some scenarios, it genuinely is.

When it IS overengineering:

If your domain has 2 or 3 stable topics that never change, creating a router with a Value Mapping adds a layer with zero return. One iFlow per topic — simple, direct, maintainable.

When it is NOT overengineering:

When the domain is alive — new business events are added frequently, topic naming evolves, or multiple teams contribute to the same domain. In that scenario:

 
Without router:  New event → new iFlow + new adapter config + new credential + deploy  
With router:  New event → 1 row in Value Mapping → done. Zero deploy. Zero code.

The Value Mapping becomes the live catalog of every event the domain knows and processes. That is not overhead — that is governance.

The question that defines the decision:

"How often do new events appear in this domain?"

Domain volatility Design
Rare — stable topicsDirect adapter per topic
Frequent — growing eventsRouter + VM · immutable iFlow
UnknownRouter + VM · safe by default

The Routing Key — Kafka Topic as Semantic Contract

Unlike IDoc routing where the key is assembled from multiple header fields, Kafka routing is simpler and more elegant. The topic name is the semantic contract. It already carries domain, subdomain, and event intent:

acme.sales.o2c.order.created
  │      │    │    │      │
  │      │    │    │      └── event: what happened
  │      │    │    └───────── detail: which process
  │      │    └────────────── subdomain: which process
  │      └─────────────────── domain: which area
  └────────────────────────── company

One field. One lookup. One result. No composite key assembly needed.

Value Mapping Configuration

Artifact: id00.o2c.vm.metadata

The AVRO Schema — Externalized by Design

The AVRO schema is stored as a CPI Externalized Parameter — not hardcoded inside the Groovy script. This is not optional — it is the only acceptable design:

Hardcoded schema  → schema changes → script change → deployment required
Externalized      → schema changes → update parameter → zero deployment

The iFlow remains immutable. The schema evolves outside it.

Configure under the iFlow Externalized Parameters:

Name  : avro
Value : { "type": "record", "name": "Order", ... }

The Groovy Scripts

Iflow routing Kafka Topic inside the Domain Layer for Sales - O2C - CI Package:

As you can see the adapter Kafka is reading all topics regards Sales - O2C - Process:

Topics:

acme.sales.o2c.order.created,acme.sales.o2c.order.confirmed,acme.sales.o2c.order.cancelled,acme.sales.o2c.invoice.created

Don't forget to add the Allowed Headers: kafka.TOPIC, this is the unic reference for the routing.

Confluence Kafka - Topics - Free Tier:

Create your own Kafka instance in 3 minutes and test it: https://www.confluent.io/get-started/

Script 1 — Router (id00.o2c.kafka.topic.routing.f.in.async)

 

// =============================================================================
//  SAP Community Blog — Draft
//  title   : Domain-Driven Kafka Routing in SAP CPI using Value Mapping
//  author  : Ricardo Viana — SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
//  version : 1.0.0
//  date    : 2026-05-22
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  pattern : SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
//  purpose : reads kafka topic header, performs vm lookup, sets target_channel
//            for processdirect routing within the same integration package
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  functions exposed:
//    processData(message)       — reads kafka topic, calls vm, sets target_channel
//    buildErrorPayload(message) — replaces body with json error on failure
// =============================================================================

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory
import com.sap.it.api.mapping.ValueMappingApi

// =============================================================================
//  function : processData
// =============================================================================
def Message processData(Message message) {

    def vm_agency_src='sales.o2c.kafka'
    def vm_id_src='kafka.topic'
    def vm_agency_tgt = 'acme.enterprise.division'
    def vm_id_tgt     = 'acme.br.pd.o2c.iflow'
    def fallback      = 'unknown'

    def props   = message.getProperties()
    def headers = message.getHeaders()

    // ── reads kafka topic from header ─────────────────────────────────────────
    def kafka_topic    = (headers['kafka.TOPIC'] as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def target_channel = fallback
    def vm_error       = ''

    try {
        def vm_api = ITApiFactory.getApi(ValueMappingApi.class, null)

        def mapped = vm_api.getMappedValue(
            vm_agency_src, vm_id_src, kafka_topic,
            vm_agency_tgt, vm_id_tgt
        )

        if (mapped?.trim()) target_channel = mapped.trim()

    } catch (Exception e) {
        vm_error = e.message ?: 'unknown error'
    }

    // ── output properties ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    props.put('target_channel', target_channel)
    props.put('is_mapped',      (target_channel != fallback).toString())
    props.put('vm_key',         kafka_topic)
    props.put('vm_error',       vm_error)
    headers.put('vm_key',       kafka_topic)

    return message
}

// =============================================================================
//  function : buildErrorPayload
// =============================================================================
def Message buildErrorPayload(Message message) {

    def props          = message.getProperties()
    def target_channel = (props['target_channel'] as String)?.trim() ?: 'unknown'
    def kafka_topic    = (props['vm_key']         as String)?.trim() ?: 'n/a'
    def vm_error       = (props['vm_error']        as String)?.trim() ?: 'none'
    def timestamp      = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    def error_map = [
        header: [
            script  : 'acme.core.script.kafka.router',
            author  : 'Ricardo Viana - SAP BTP Integration Expert',
            severity: 'error'
        ],
        input: [
            kafka_topic: kafka_topic
        ],
        routing: [
            timestamp     : timestamp,
            vm_key        : kafka_topic,
            target_channel: target_channel,
            is_mapped     : false,
            vm_artifact   : 'acme.kafka.domain',
            vm_error      : vm_error
        ],
        action_required: "Register topic '${kafka_topic}' in value mapping 'acme.kafka.domain'"
    ]

    message.setBody(
        groovy.json.JsonOutput.prettyPrint(
            groovy.json.JsonOutput.toJson(error_map)
        )
    )
    message.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json')

    return message
}

Iflow processing topic inside the package Sales O2C for the topic as sample

Kafka Topic for: acme.sales.o2c.order.created - Iflow to process: id01.o2c.kafka.order.created

Script 2 — Processing Iflow (id01.o2c.kafka.order.created)

I'm going to left to possibilities of output as JSON or XML than you change as per of your needs, the sample I used XML as output because of that JSON is commented.

// =============================================================================
//  SAP Community Blog
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  title   : Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI
//            — Domain-Driven Event Routing for Sales O2C Topics
//  author  : Ricardo Viana — SAP BTP Integration Architect | SDIA Creator
//  series  : SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
//  pattern : ODCP — Operational Domain Contract Pattern
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  abstract:
//    This script demonstrates how to implement a domain-scoped Kafka topic
//    router inside SAP Cloud Integration using the SDIA governance model.
//    The AVRO deserialization is decoupled from the input source — the same
//    logic handles raw bytes from the Kafka Sender Adapter, Base64, or Hex,
//    with only a single line change depending on the scenario.
//    The AVRO schema is externalized via CPI Externalized Parameters,
//    making the iFlow immutable — no code change required when the schema
//    evolves.
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  related artifacts:
//    value mapping : acme.kafka.domain / acme.kafka.topic
//    ext parameter : avro (AVRO schema JSON)
//    iflow package : ACME.Sales.O2C
// =============================================================================

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import org.apache.avro.Schema
import org.apache.avro.generic.GenericDatumReader
import org.apache.avro.generic.GenericRecord
import org.apache.avro.io.BinaryDecoder
import org.apache.avro.io.DecoderFactory
import groovy.json.JsonBuilder
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder
import java.io.ByteArrayInputStream

def Message processData(Message message) {

    // ── 1. INPUT — choose according to your scenario ──────────────────────────
    //
    //  PRODUCTION (Kafka Sender Adapter — raw bytes):
        byte[] rawBytes = message.getBody(byte[].class)
    //
    //  SIMULATION — Base64 string (e.g. Content Modifier):
    //  byte[] rawBytes =  Base64.getDecoder().decode(message.getBody(String.class).trim())
    //
    //  SIMULATION — Hex string (e.g. Content Modifier):
    //  def hex = message.getBody(String.class).trim().replaceAll("\\s+", "")
    //  byte[] rawBytes = hex.replaceAll("[^0-9a-fA-F]", "").toList().collate(2)
    //                       .collect { it.join() }
    //                       .collect { Integer.parseInt(it, 16) as byte }
    //                       .toArray(new byte[0])
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    // ── Confluent wire format detection ───────────────────────────────────────
    // Confluent prepends: [ 0x00 magic ][ 4 bytes schema ID ][ avro payload ]
    // If detected, strip the first 5 bytes before deserializing.
    byte[] avroBytes
    if (rawBytes[0] == 0x00) {
        avroBytes = rawBytes[5..-1] as byte[]
    } else {
        avroBytes = rawBytes
    }
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    // ── 2. Schema via Externalized Parameter (iFlow immutable) ────────────────
    def schemaStr = message.getProperty("avro").toString()

    // ── 3. Deserialize AVRO → GenericRecord ───────────────────────────────────
    Schema schema = new Schema.Parser().parse(schemaStr)
    ByteArrayInputStream bais = new ByteArrayInputStream(avroBytes)
    BinaryDecoder decoder = DecoderFactory.get().binaryDecoder(bais, null)
    GenericDatumReader reader = new GenericDatumReader(schema)
    GenericRecord decoded = (GenericRecord) reader.read(null, decoder)

    /*// ── 4. GenericRecord → JSON ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    def builder = new JsonBuilder()
    builder {
        orderId     decoded.get("orderId").toString()
        customerId  decoded.get("customerId").toString()
        totalAmount decoded.get("totalAmount")
        currency    decoded.get("currency").toString()
        status      decoded.get("status").toString()
        createdAt   decoded.get("createdAt")
        items       decoded.get("items").collect { item -> [
            sku     : item.get("sku").toString(),
            quantity: item.get("quantity"),
            price   : item.get("price")
        ]}
    }*/
    
    // ── 5. GenericRecord → XML ────────────────────────────────────────────────
    Writer writer = new StringWriter()
    def indentPrinter = new IndentPrinter(writer, ' ')
    def builder = new MarkupBuilder(indentPrinter)

    
    builder.Order {
        orderId     decoded.get("orderId").toString()
        customerId  decoded.get("customerId").toString()
        totalAmount decoded.get("totalAmount")
        currency    decoded.get("currency").toString()
        status      decoded.get("status").toString()
        createdAt   decoded.get("createdAt")
        Items {
            decoded.get("items").each { item ->
                Item {
                    sku      item.get("sku").toString()
                    quantity item.get("quantity")
                    price    item.get("price")
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    message.setBody(writer.toString())
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/xml")

    //message.setBody(builder.toPrettyString())
    //message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

    return message
}

Required JAR

Upload to iFlow under Resources → Archives (JAR):

 

Library Version Source
avro-1.12.1.jar1.12.1https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/org/apache/avro/avro/1.12.1/
 

Sample of avro and schema

 Dinary base64 sample input to Kafka Topic

{
  "value": {
    "type": "BINARY",
    "data": "HE9SRC0yMDI0LTAwMTIzEENVU1QtNDU2AAAAAABzl0AGQlJMAICgq4f0YwQOTUFULTAwMQYAAAAAAOR1QA5NQVQtMDAyAgAAAAAAIHxAAA=="
  }
}

 Avro Schema:

{
  "type": "record",
  "name": "Order",
  "namespace": "com.example.sap",
  "fields": [
    {
      "name": "orderId",
      "type": "string"
    },
    {
      "name": "customerId",
      "type": "string"
    },
    {
      "name": "totalAmount",
      "type": "double"
    },
    {
      "name": "currency",
      "type": "string"
    },
    {
      "name": "status",
      "type": {
        "type": "enum",
        "name": "OrderStatus",
        "symbols": [
          "CREATED",
          "CONFIRMED",
          "CANCELLED"
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "name": "createdAt",
      "type": "long"
    },
    {
      "name": "items",
      "type": {
        "type": "array",
        "items": {
          "type": "record",
          "name": "OrderItem",
          "fields": [
            {
              "name": "sku",
              "type": "string"
            },
            {
              "name": "quantity",
              "type": "int"
            },
            {
              "name": "price",
              "type": "double"
            }
          ]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

Postman:

Result in XML:

Before and After

 

  Traditional This pattern

New event typeNew iFlow + adapter + deployAdd 1 VM row
New topicNew iFlow + credential + deployAdd 1 VM row
Schema changeEdit Groovy + deployUpdate Externalized Parameter
Routing logicHardcoded per iFlowValue Mapping only
TraceabilityPer-iFlow MPLvm_key in every MPL log
iFlow count (5 events)5 iFlows with adapters1 router + 5 processing

 

Conclusion

The Kafka adapter never changes. The Value Mapping grows. The schema never touches the iFlow. The Externalized Parameter evolves.

Applying SDIA and ODCP to SAP Cloud Integration, you eliminate three of the most common failure modes in enterprise integration landscapes:

  • Package Sprawl → one package per domain. period.
  • Naming Chaos → every artifact carries the domain token. no exceptions.
  • Artifact Sprawl → one router iFlow per domain. processing iFlows are templates. Value Mapping is the only thing that grows.

Same principle. Layer 3 (ODCP) of the same architecture — SDIA.

SDIA and ODCP are open conceptual patterns created to reduce artifact sprawl and improve domain governance across SAP Integration Suite landscapes.

When your Sales O2C domain adds a new event type — acme.sales.o2c.shipment.dispatched — the steps are:

  1. Create the processing iFlow id01.o2c.kafka.shipment.dispatched
  2. Add one row to id00.o2c.vm.metadata
  3. Deploy the processing iFlow only.

The router iFlow id00.o2c.kafka.topic.routing.f.in.async is never touched.That is the contract.

This is not a CPI trick. This is not overengineering. This is what governance looks like when the domain is the primary key —
and every artifact knows exactly what it owns and what it does not.

  • The iFlow is the contract.
  • The Value Mapping is the configuration.
  • The domain is the boundary.
  • They must never be confused.
Package
All Iflows for Sales O2c independently of the tecnology should be store here.
└── acme.br.sales.o2c.i
     │    │    │    │ │
     │    │    │    │ └──── integrations
     │    │    │    └─────────── subdomain: which process
     │    │    └──────────────── domain: Main Contract
     │    └───────────────────── division: which area
     └────────────────────────── company

    iFlow — Receiver/Router (Layer 1 — Immutable)
    └── id00.o2c.kafka.topic.routing.f.in.async

    iFlows — Processing (Layer 2 — Domain Owned)
    ├── id01.o2c.kafka.order.created.f.in.async
    ├── id01.o2c.kafka.order.confirmed.f.in.async
    ├── id01.o2c.kafka.order.cancelled.f.in.async
    └── id01.o2c.kafka.invoice.created.f.in.async

    Value Mapping
    └── id00.o2c.vm.metadata

    Script Collection
    └── id00.o2c.sc.kafka.router

    Security Material
    └── cr.sales.o2c.basic.kafka

You see SALES everywhere now ? So start to apply this 
semantic domain-centric contract and you solve a 
lot of governance problems in your company.

Resources

  • 📄ODCP v3.0: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593
  • 📄SDIA v3.0: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635
  • 🌐site: www.domain-intent.com

entry.title: Re: Object RM07MIGO_PO_HISTORY of class RE does not exist...
entry.content:
Hello!

The error message at the bottom of the screen: Object RM07MIGO_PO_HISTORY of class RE does not exist in language EN, Message no. SD140.

Class RE in SAP refers to an ABAP Report/Program object. This message means the system is attempting to call the report RM07MIGO_PO_HISTORY when the "History" button is clicked in the Purchase Order Data tab of the material document, but this program either does not exist in the system or does not have its text elements maintained for language EN (English) in this environment.

Two important observations from the screenshot:

  • Informational message, not a hard error. The status bar icon is green, meaning this is an informational/warning message (SD140), not a blocking error. The goods receipt document itself has been posted successfully. The error only affects the ability to open the PO History popup from within the material document display.
  • Brazil localization context. The toolbar shows the "Nota Fiscal" button and line 2 shows storage location "RECEBIMENTO" (Portuguese for "receipt"), confirming this is a Brazil-localized STO scenario. This is relevant because SAP has specific known issues with STO movement types in Brazilian localization (see SAP KBA 2935010 — Error MIGO 060 in Stock Transfer Order scenario in Brazil), and the localization layer may affect which programs are available or active in the system.

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the program RM07MIGO_PO_HISTORY is the underlying report called by the History button to display the purchase order history popup within MIGO. In the Public Edition environment this program may not be activated, not translated for the logon language, or not within the standard delivered scope for STO goods receipt documents.

Workaround:

The PO history is accessible via alternative standard paths:

Option 1: Via the "Manage Purchase Orders" App. Search for the relevant Stock Transfer Order (STO) number. Open the PO and navigate to the "Purchase Order History" tab at item level. This displays all GR, GI, and invoice documents linked to the STO item with full quantities and values.

Option 2: Via the "Manage Goods Movements" / "Display Material Document" app. Navigate to the material document and use the document flow / reference document links to trace the full STO process chain (outbound delivery → goods issue → goods receipt).

entry.title: Unlock Better Hiring with Qualifications: Start Smart, Scale Faster with SAP Fieldglass
entry.content:
Why Qualifications Matter

Qualifications bring structure and consistency to hiring by replacing free-text job requirements with clearly defined skills. When used consistently, they improve candidate matching, enable better system recommendations, and create a reusable framework that scales with your organization. Without this structure, job postings vary widely and AI features lack the context needed to be effective.

The Hidden Cost of Skipping Day 1 Setup

Features like Recommend Qualifications depend entirely on an existing qualification library. Without it, the system has no reference point, and recommendations cannot work. Creating this library manually can feel time-consuming, which is why many teams delay it—and often never return to it.

The Solution: Generate Qualifications from Existing Data

Instead of starting from scratch, you can use Generate Qualifications to build your library instantly using AI. The system analyzes your existing job postings and templates, extracts commonly used skills, and creates a recommended list of qualifications. In one step, you move from having no structure to a usable, standardized framework.

What You Need to Do (Day One Setup)

  1. Upload your job postings or templates
  2. Contact your Configuration Manager to run Generate Qualifications
  3. Review and select the relevant qualifications

This creates a ready-to-use qualification library that supports both job postings and recommendations.

Activating Recommend Qualifications

Once your library is in place, enable the Recommend Qualifications feature. During job posting creation, the system suggests relevant qualifications based on your job description—using your own structured data.

Key insight: The quality of recommendations depends on the quality of your library.

How This Improves Hiring

With qualifications in place, recruiters can create consistent job postings, rely on better recommendations, and improve candidate matching. This leads to faster, more accurate hiring decisions without adding complexity.

Final Thoughts

Qualifications are a foundational step in skill-based hiring. By setting them up early using Generate Qualifications, you can quickly unlock the value of AI features and create a more consistent, scalable hiring process.

Start with the data you already have, build your qualification library, and let the system do the rest.

You can also have a look at the following topic in the What’s New Viewer for SAP Fieldglass for more details: 

AI Assisted Qualification Generation for Qualification Library

entry.title: Re: Statement not appearing correctly when using Personal...
entry.content: Hello @xavierlegarrec, In the meanwhile we have managed to fix the issue. The preview and accessing the link via link in notification where showing a comment that was in the xml whilst the downloaded statement did not show that comment (as it should). It is quite strange that a comment in the xml was appearing in the preview because that code should not be considered, as comments in xml should never be considered. The download was not considering it as expected. As we managed to solve the issue (we deleted the comment from XML) I have sent an email to SAP to delete this post as it is resolved. Thank you for your help!

entry.title: Re: Credit Management SSCUIs with "1QM, BD6" scope refere...
entry.content:
Hello!

Both hypotheses are partially correct. The accurate picture is a combination of the two: all 5 SSCUI are shared infrastructure used by both BD6 and 1QM, but they serve different depths of functionality. For BD6-only, all 5 are applicable and most are required to make the credit check work at all, they are not 1QM-exclusive. The distinction is that 1QM adds automation on top of the same configuration foundation.

Questions answers:

Q1. Which SSCUIs Are Required for BD6-Only?

All 5 SSCUIs listed are applicable and relevant in a BD6-only scenario. The fact that they reference both 1QM and BD6 in the Scope Reference column confirms they are shared configuration, not 1QM-exclusive.

SSCUI 101508: Assign Sales Area to Credit Segment: Required. You need to maintain the credit segment for the sales area in SSCUI 101508 so that a newly created sales order will be checked against the correct credit segment. Without this, credit checks on sales orders will either fail or start against the wrong segment.  https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/message-from-credit-check-sap-credit-management-failed-against-the-wrong/qaq-p/12302182

SSCUI 101518: Define Automatic Credit Control: Required. This is the core SSCUI that defines the system reaction when a credit limit is exceeded,warning, error, or block. Without it, the system does not know how to respond to a credit breach.

SSCUI 101850:  Define Customer Credit Group: Required. Credit groups classify sales documents and deliveries for the credit check. They must be assigned to sales document types and delivery types. Without them, the credit check cannot be triggered correctly at the document level.

SSCUI 102623:  Define Checking Rules: Required. Checking rules define which credit check steps are active, such as the credit limit check, oldest open item check, or maximum dunning level check. This drives what BD6 evaluates during a credit check. Without a correctly assigned checking rule, no check steps will execute.

SSCUI 102570: Define Credit Groups: Required. This is a prerequisite for 101850 and 101518. The credit groups must exist before they can be assigned to document types and referenced in automatic credit control.

SAP KBA 3571923 confirms that both SSCUI 101508 and SSCUI 101518 are directly implicated when a credit check fails to trigger on a sales order. https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3571923

 

Q2. Functional Difference Between BD6 and 1QM

Basic Credit Management (BD6) is part of the core ERP and is included in the standard subscription. Advanced Credit Management (1QM) refers to more advanced functionality that requires additional licensing, allowing the business to categorise customers according to scoring rules, define those scoring rules, and automatically calculate and assign customers specific credit limits. https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/grow-with-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-premium-a-practical-guide/ba-p/13953369

Under BD6, the following is available: credit limit check on sales orders, manual credit limit maintenance on the Business Partner, oldest open item and dunning level checks, and credit block with one-step release by the credit controller. The credit segment, checking rules, credit groups, and automatic credit control configuration are all part of BD6 and must be configured to make the process work.

Under 1QM, everything in BD6 remains, and the following is added on top: automatic credit score calculation based on configurable scoring rules, automatic credit limit calculation using formula-based logic, a credit limit request and approval workflow via the "Manage Credit Limit Requests" app, and the ability to integrate with external credit agencies via scope item 1RY.

Only manual update of the credit limit is available in the Basic Credit Management scope item BD6. For advanced functions, the 1QM scope item must be activated, which provides the credit limit request workflow via the "Manage Credit Limit Requests" app. https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3351046

 

Q3.  Impact of configuring or not configuring SSCUIs Under BD6

If you do not configure these SSCUIs, the credit check will not trigger on sales orders or will fire inconsistently. BD6 checks the exposure against the current credit limit for each business partner, and when a new order is blocked due to exceeding the credit limit, an authorized staff member can release or reject it. This process cannot work if the underlying configuration is missing. Sales orders for customers who have exceeded their credit limit will be processed without any block. https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/manage-your-business-partner-s-creditworthiness-with-basic-credit/ba-p/13542774

A missing or wrong credit segment assignment in SSCUI 101508 means the check fires against the wrong segment or the default segment 0000, potentially ignoring company-code-specific credit limits.

entry.title: The future of data is conversational and agentic AI — Are you ready?
entry.content:
Hey Data Professional Community  👋

As AI assistants become more embedded in data workflows, many of us are starting to rethink how day-to-day work gets done. 

🤔  We are curious your thoughts on:

  • How AI will or can impact your role as a data professional?
  • Where do you see real gains and where does it still fall short?
  • Any concerns about trusting AI with critical tasks?

To get you thinking, here are some of the agentic AI features planned for SAP BDC, leveraging Joule:

  • Data Product Search & Creation — Natural language replaces manual, multi-step builds
  • Automated Planning & Analytical Models — Describe it, Joule builds it
  • Business Insights for Everyone — Complex questions answered without SQL
  • AI-Assisted Story Generation — Executive-ready dashboards through simple conversation

Drop your thoughts!

entry.title: Re: Deep Insert in SAP CAPM Using Composition and Fiori Elements
entry.content: Very practical and timely. The deep insert with composition is exactly the kind of pattern people need when working with nested entities in CAP + Fiori Elements, and it’s great to see how CAP handles the whole payload in one transaction. I like that the post shows both the CDS model and the UI side, since that makes it much easier to understand how the composition and deep insert fit together end to end.

entry.title: Re: Securing SAP UI5 Applications with a Custom Node.js Backend using JWT
entry.content:
Really solid write‑up. I’ve seen quite a few teams pair UI5 with a lightweight Node.js backend when they don’t want the overhead of BTP or XSUAA, and security is always the part that gets hand‑waved until the last minute. Your end‑to‑end walkthrough fills that gap nicely.

What I liked most is how practical the flow is — from hashing passwords to generating JWTs to wiring up the middleware and handling token expiry on the UI5 side. A lot of examples online skip the real‑world pieces like role‑based routing, proxy setup in ui5.yaml, or how to structure the backend cleanly. You covered all of that in a way that’s easy to follow.

This is the kind of content that helps UI5 developers step outside the SAP bubble and adopt modern security patterns without over‑engineering the solution. Great job putting this together.

entry.title: Re: Tapping into your organization's resourcefulness with SAP Funds Management
entry.content: Great perspective. The post does a good job of showing how Funds Management is not just about applying budgets, but about tapping into organizational resourcefulness by making budget visibility and commitment control part of everyday decisions. I like that it connects FM to real business outcomes like better planning, fewer overruns, and more disciplined spending, which makes the topic feel very relevant for both public sector and any organization with tight budget control.

entry.title: Re: Blog#1: Finally it is here - How "pony" brings custom fields to SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Really interesting read. I’ve been following the evolution of custom fields across S/4HANA and BTP for a while, and this “Pony” approach feels like the missing piece a lot of teams have been waiting for. The way you explained how it brings consistency, governance, and a cleaner extension model across the stack really resonated with me.

What I appreciate most is that you didn’t just describe the feature — you showed the practical impact. Anyone who has dealt with scattered custom fields, inconsistent naming, or integration headaches knows how valuable a unified model can be. This is the kind of improvement that quietly solves a lot of long‑standing pain points in real projects.

Thanks for putting this together. It’s great to see SAP investing in making extensibility more predictable and developer‑friendly.

entry.title: Re: How to Restrict CRUD Operations in SAP CAPM Using @Capabilities, @readonly and Test APIs with .h
entry.content: Very useful and practical. The post clearly explains how to restrict CRUD operations in CAP using annotations like @readonly, @insertonly, and @restrict, and shows how they translate into capability restrictions at the OData level. I especially like that it connects the model-level annotations to real authorization behavior, which makes it easier to implement fine-grained access control without overcomplicating the service.

entry.title: Re: The Patient SAP Technical Architect
entry.content:
Really enjoyed reading this perspective. The article captures something many SAP professionals experience but rarely talk about openly — enterprise transformation is not just about technology, timelines, or architecture diagrams. It’s also about patience, communication, and guiding organizations through constant change.

I especially liked how the role of a technical architect was described beyond system design. In real SAP S/4HANA programs, architects often become the bridge between business expectations, technical realities, integration challenges, and long-term scalability decisions.

The healthcare examples also make the topic more relatable because modernization in healthcare environments requires both technical precision and operational sensitivity. That balance is not easy to achieve.

A thoughtful and realistic article for anyone working in SAP transformation programs.

entry.title: Re: I tricked Datasphere into running my custom scaler functions
entry.content:
This was a fun read. Anyone who has spent time inside Datasphere knows how opinionated it can be about what you’re “allowed” to run, so seeing you push the boundaries with custom scaler functions was both entertaining and genuinely insightful. The way you broke down the trick, the limitations, and the practical use cases made it easy to follow without overselling it.

What I really appreciated is that you treated this as an exploration rather than a magic solution. A lot of us have wondered how far we can stretch Datasphere’s transformation layer without jumping out to external engines, and your experiment gives a clear sense of what’s possible today — and where the guardrails still are.

Thanks for sharing this. It’s the kind of hands‑on curiosity that moves the platform forward and helps the rest of us think a little more creatively about what Datasphere can do.

entry.title: Re: XML Annotations: The Essentials Before Adapting Documentation Samples to Your Project
entry.content:
Really appreciated this post. XML annotations are one of those topics that look simple when you read SAP’s documentation, but the moment you try to adapt a sample to a real project, all the hidden rules and constraints start showing up. Your breakdown of the essentials — especially the parts about naming, binding contexts, and how annotations behave differently depending on where they’re defined — is exactly the kind of clarity developers need.

I also liked that you focused on the “why,” not just the “how.” Understanding the intent behind the annotation structure makes it much easier to troubleshoot when something doesn’t render the way the sample suggests. This is the kind of practical guidance that saves hours of trial and error.

Thanks for putting this together. It’s a solid reference for anyone trying to bridge the gap between SAP’s examples and real‑world UI5 development.

I especially like that it focuses on a concrete use case—automating deployment tasks end-to-end—rather than just talking about automation in general, which makes it much easier to see how this would fit into a DevOps workflow.
